chapters.title: Why old wounds still bleed into modern America
chapters.text:
In 2017, New Orleans became the stage for an unusual scene. At night, trucks and cranes rolled up to the monuments in the heart of the city. Drivers hid their license plates and the names of their companies. When workers climbed out of the vehicles, nothing marked their identity, not even a logo on a uniform. They wore masks. Were they up to something criminal?

No, they were carrying out the mayor's orders. After deciding to remove monuments honoring Confederates, defenders of slavery during the Civil War, the mayor met fierce resistance. One by one, contractors refused to work with the city. The reason was a wave of anonymous pro-Confederate threats against construction companies, including a firebombing of one contractor's car. The mayor found only one firm willing to help New Orleans, and it was run by African Americans.

In the end, workers removed all of the statues, and the last one even came down in broad daylight. The crowd cheered the result. It looked like a clear victory of democracy over slavery, a belief most Americans claim to support. So what was behind the resistance? It traces back to the caste system of the past. Americans may doubt the term, but it was obvious to Indians.

Historical knowledge is essential for understanding any social challenge in the United States.

Martin Luther King and his wife traveled to India in 1959. They had dinner with Jawaharlal Nehru, the prime minister. The couple then visited a school for children of the Untouchables, the Dalits. They sat at the bottom of a five-caste system in which even their shadows could not fall on those with higher status. When the school principal introduced King to the pupils, he called King an Untouchable. The famous American was startled, but on reflection he agreed the description fit.

Are you ready to see how the caste idea sheds light on American success and on Trump's victory? Let us investigate together.

chapters.title: The selfish logic behind every caste system
chapters.text:
The idea of castes in the United States is not new. Senator Charles Sumner, who lived from 1811 to 1874, was among the first to mention it. Although some scholars picked up the term, others argued that the caste system was unique to India. Part of that belief rested on the assumption that Dalits, unlike African Americans, accepted their place in the hierarchy, and that the order was natural.

That view of India rested on stereotypes. The truth was the opposite. Untouchables fought hard for their rights and took inspiration from the American civil rights movement. In the 1970s, they founded the Dalit Panthers, named after the Black Panther Party in the United States. The parallel between the two countries was well known to Untouchables. It was not just them. In 1934, the Nazis studied how American laws banned marriages across racial lines so they could apply a similar idea to keep Aryan blood pure.

So what exactly does caste mean, and why does it apply to American society? Its defining feature is permanence. People cannot change their place in the hierarchy. Caste is to a community what grammar is to a language. Grammar sets the position of words in a sentence and the rules of behavior in different contexts. Caste, in the same way, sets people's status and limits, defining who has power, resources, and respect.

Recognize and celebrate the value of diverse cultures, languages, and traditions.

A caste is a social construct built to reinforce domination. That definition lets us see more historical parallels. India had five castes and the United States had two, but Untouchables and African Americans sat at the bottom of each. Those at the top of both hierarchies mistreated indigenous peoples, forcing Native Americans and Adivasis in India off their lands. In both countries, laws legitimized the unfair order, protecting the interests of those at the top. Lower castes put in huge effort to win equal rights. Martin Luther King and Bhimrao Ambedkar led the protest movements in the United States and India. In 1964, President Lyndon B. Johnson ended segregation with the Civil Rights Act. A little over a decade earlier, India had banned discrimination based on caste. Although the legal walls of inequality came down, the walls inside people's minds have only partly fallen.

The caste systems in the United States, India, and Nazi Germany shared the same core principles, which we will turn to next.

chapters.title: The eight pillars that prop up a caste system
chapters.text:
Isabel Wilkerson has laid out eight core principles of caste. They reinforced the division of society and helped the privileged hold on to the status quo. Let us look at these ideas through examples from American and Indian history.

First, a higher position meant being superior. That is the basic idea of any hierarchy. A developed system of symbols and rituals aimed to sustain this principle. For example, the lower caste in both India and the United States was expected to step off the sidewalk when someone from a higher level walked by.

Second, the social hierarchy was presented as something sent from above. No one group could be blamed for it. Since the Middle Ages, the higher caste in the West leaned on the Bible story of Canaan, the son of Ham. Noah told him that his descendants would serve others. In some Western readings, Ham's family was placed in the South and was said to have black skin. In the same way, Indian castes appeared in an ancient Hindu text, which may be why Ambedkar later became a Buddhist.

Third, people inherited their caste from their parents. In the United States, the mother's status was the deciding factor. The reason was simple. Otherwise, members of the higher caste would have had to treat their children with enslaved women as equals. In India, the father's rank in the hierarchy decided the child's caste.

Fourth, parents passed down more than status. Their occupation was almost set from birth. In 1890, most African Americans worked in agriculture or domestic service, around 85 percent of men and 96 percent of women. Even in the North, it was rare for lower-caste people to find a position higher than what was available in the South. In India, certain families held the same occupation for centuries.

The fifth pillar lays the foundation for the next three we will discuss, and it is endogamy. It banned people from marrying outside their caste. Its effects reached well beyond the lack of romantic interest between groups. It meant society could not build bonds across the lines or feel sympathy strong enough to push for change. The rule also kept resources locked within a single caste.

Speak up against racism and discrimination whenever you witness it.

In 1967, the Supreme Court struck down laws that banned marriages between people of different races, forcing the sixteen remaining states to remove that relic of the past.

chapters.title: Once empathy dies, devaluation is not far behind
chapters.text:
A low place on the hierarchy did not just shut people out of education, decent work, and respect. It could also devalue their lives. The mass killings in Nazi Germany are the most extreme example of that logic.

To help restore a sense of justice, the German artist Gunter Demnig started a project in 1992 called Stolperstein, which translates loosely as stumbling stone. Small concrete cubes with a brass plate are set into the pavement near the homes of victims. Each plate shows a person's name, date of birth, and fate. It is a powerful way of showing that the victims, whom the Nazis reduced to numbers, had real identities, and that we owe it to them to remember. The project also aims to nurture genuine compassion in everyone who walks past these Stolpersteins.

The higher caste of the past did the opposite. First, it painted the lower caste as a threat to the purity of those at the top. This is the sixth pillar. In India, Dalits had to stay a certain number of steps away from others, between 12 and 96 depending on the region and the era. In some periods, they had to sweep the ground in front of them so they would leave no footprints. Objects touched by lower-caste people were considered polluted. Sounds strange to a Western reader? It was not strange everywhere.

Regularly reflect on your own biases and work to overcome them.

In North Carolina, children of different castes used different textbooks at the end of the nineteenth century. In Florida, those books were not even shelved together. In 1931, when a member of an unprivileged group came to a public pool in Pittsburgh, members of the higher caste were outraged. The hypocrisy is striking. No one mentioned pollution when a low-caste person cooked a meal for the privileged or cared for their children.

This attitude set the stage for the seventh pillar, which is dehumanization. On arriving in the United States, enslaved people were forced to accept new names. In India, Dalits were given surnames that marked their status.

Terror, the eighth principle, was the main tool for keeping the status quo. During the era of enslavement, whipping was a common practice in the United States, and the forms of torture were so widespread that they even had names to tell them apart.

chapters.title: Caste lives on, dressed in new clothes
chapters.text:
When colonists arrived in the United States, they were set on profiting at any cost. They soon found a tool for that aim, which was slavery. It lasted in the country from 1619 to 1865, a stretch of nearly 250 years. Tragically, the very strengths of African Americans helped to lock them into bondage, including their discipline and cooperation, their immunity to local diseases, their quickness to learn, and their deep experience in agriculture, especially with crops new to Europeans.

As researchers Audrey and Brian Smedley have noted, the colonists soon realized that without the Africans and the skills they brought, their enterprises would fail.

At first, plantation owners justified slavery by claiming that African Americans were not Christians. Once conversions became common, the colonists reached for a new tool, which was caste.

Imagine a world in which people with blue eyes held every privilege while people with brown eyes were servants. Sounds absurd, does it not? Why should a detail that small decide anyone's fate? Yet that is exactly what the colonists did, using skin color as the marker.

Slavery turned human beings into property and currency. On arrival in the United States, families were torn apart, since wives, husbands, and children could belong to different owners. A small measure of corn might be all the food they had for a week, and violence was everywhere. Plantations were labor camps. South Carolina and other states set a daily work norm in 1740 of 14 to 15 hours, which was twice as long as the workday of free laborers. Even prisoners worked fewer hours at the time.

The situation in the slaveholding states did shift after the Civil War, which ran from 1861 to 1865, and new laws restored some justice for African Americans. Yet when federal troops pulled out of the South in 1877, the Reconstruction period ended. The fate of formerly enslaved people fell back into the hands of their former owners.

We tend to think of caste as a question of the distant past. Yet lynchings, the killing of suspects without trial, often by hanging, still took place in the South in the early years of the twentieth century.

Only 70 years ago, buses in the American South had separate sections for white and Black passengers.

Educate the next generation. Teach children about diversity, equality, and inclusion.

In Texas in the 1970s, it was rare to hear anyone call an African American Mister or Miss. Most people addressed them by first name only. That is why one man started calling his daughter Miss, as a quiet act of protest.

The situation has improved enormously since then, but inequality has found fresh, more sophisticated, and more hidden ways to leak into society.

chapters.title: The stereotypes that screens and culture keep feeding us
chapters.text:
Racism is often associated with open anger toward other groups. The most common form, though, is unconscious, and it shows up as bias. Prejudices, mocking, and intolerant jokes are a few of its faces.

Police calls show the power of these stereotypes in a vivid way. How often have citizens treated others as suspicious because of their skin color? One woman once called the police to report that a man had kidnapped two children. Her assumption rested on the fact that the children were white and the man was not. The man turned out to be their babysitter. Can you imagine the same accusation being aimed at someone from a previously higher caste?

Representation in the media is a key force in reinforcing stereotypes. According to a researcher from the University of Illinois, 59 percent of the low-income people in the news are Black. That share is far out of step with reality, since only about 22 percent of African Americans earn low incomes.

The pattern shows up in crime coverage too. When the culprit is Black and the victim is white, those cases make up 42 percent of all crime reports in the news, while the Sentencing Project puts the real share at around 10 percent.

Educate yourself through documentaries that explore racism, inequality, and social justice.

At the same time, the share of African American teenagers who became mothers dropped sharply. According to research by Child Trends, the rate fell from 118 to 28 per 1,000, one of the lowest figures in modern times. You will rarely read about that in the media.

Culture can also feed stereotypes. Do you remember the film Gone with the Wind? Hattie McDaniel played a loyal servant who paid more attention to the people she worked for than to her own family. The role reinforced stereotypes, yet it also made McDaniel the first African American to win an Academy Award. Watch the series, games, and comedy shows around you, and you will see the roots of prejudice. These negative messages leave a heavy mark.

According to the Harvard sociologist David R. Williams, biases are so widespread that 30 percent of Black people believe these stereotypes about themselves.

chapters.title: How bias drains our health and our promise
chapters.text:
The cost of caste biases is high. Let us look at two of their biggest consequences.

First, a tremendous loss of potential. Experts called LeRoy Satchel Paige one of the best baseball pitchers in history. He threw so hard that catchers' hands burned after the game. Many said he was the fastest pitcher they had ever seen. Yet he could not play in the major leagues until 1946, when he was already forty years old. Even at that age, he showed brilliance and proved his reputation as a living legend. Still, baseball lost a player at the peak of his powers because of segregation.

Sometimes it was not just about talent. In 1721, Boston faced a smallpox epidemic. An enslaved African named Onesimus told his owner, Cotton Mather, about the methods used in his homeland. Mather trusted him and tried to spread the word. He ran into fierce resistance, and someone even threw a lit grenade into his house. Yet one physician believed in the method. Soon it became standard practice.

Einstein once said that the separation of the races is not a disease of the colored people, but a disease of the white people.

Second, higher risks for health. Prejudice and unrelenting challenges speed up the aging of telomeres, which are the protective caps on chromosomes. It affects the longevity of those who are discriminated against. Wealthier low-caste members are often hit hardest, since the higher they climb, the more hostility they face. The impact of bias on low-income African Americans and Mexican immigrants is somewhat lower.

Discrimination also raises the risk of diabetes and stroke for those who are targets of stereotypes. The reason is simple. Bias makes the brain treat the person as a threat, and the amygdala reacts to that signal. In extreme situations, that response keeps us alive. In ordinary life, it wears down the immune system and damages the heart.

People can avoid these harmful results if they are aware of their stereotypes. Concentrating on personal traits rather than a group image also helps. To do that, though, it is essential to have positive experiences of cooperation, which means creating more chances for groups to work side by side. It will also show that both groups can reach incredible results through partnership.

Attend cultural festivals and events to learn about and celebrate different cultures.

chapters.title: Politics in the present is rooted in old patterns
chapters.text:
Trump's victory puzzled many experts around the world. For Isabel Wilkerson, though, it was logical. According to research, implicit biases against African Americans rose from 49 to 56 percent during Obama's first term. Wilkerson explains that through the lens of caste. Members of a higher caste can see the success of a lower caste as a threat to their own place. There have been precedents in the past.

Some experts argue that Democrats paid a heavy price for signing the Civil Rights Act in 1964 and for supporting African Americans. Some say they have lost the South, but Wilkerson suggests that is an underestimation. Everyone who wanted to keep the old order in place chose the Republicans. As a result, since 1964, the United States has had only three Democratic presidents: Jimmy Carter, Bill Clinton, and Barack Obama.

Wilkerson believes the caste question was central to Trump's support. The numbers show that he was the leading candidate for nearly every category of white voters. Only white voters with low incomes were an exception.

Every act of understanding is a victory over the shadows of ignorance.

Other factors also mattered, including foreign interference and social media. On top of that, Republicans put huge effort into restricting categories of voters. The Brennan Center for Justice reported that states changed their laws in ways that blocked around 16 million people from casting a ballot. That hit marginalized people and immigrants hardest, and they tend to vote Democratic. For example, new voter ID rules became a real obstacle for many of them.

Whatever the main reason, we need more than tolerance today. We should be openly pro-African American, pro-woman, pro-Asian, and so on. Americans can learn from Albert Einstein. When he moved to the United States, he drew public attention to lynchings until they finally stopped.

Everyone can make a difference, and that brings a glimmer of hope. The deep transformation of American society shows that the caste problem has lost some of its grip. It proves that people can restore justice and push back against harmful stereotypes. The first step is to be aware of biases and to keep them from shaping our attitudes and behavior.

chapters.title: Final thoughts
chapters.text:
No one is immune to a caste mindset. We constantly compare ourselves to others to make sure we are keeping up. That does not mean we are doomed to stay biased. Our commitment to keeping discrimination out of our assumptions is the deciding factor. We can build positive attitudes by getting to know others better. In doing so, we will discover many personal stories that push stereotypes to the side.

Cooperation is essential, but so is facing unconscious fears. Within two decades, the United States will have more people who are not of European descent than people who are white. That projection may be one of the strongest concerns for the former higher caste. Some may see it as the ground shifting under their feet. They start to project their fears of job loss or other social problems onto African Americans or migrants.

Yet those people are not the cause of the problem. Millennials, born between 1981 and 2000, generally earn the same as or less than their parents. That is a trend in many Western countries. On top of that, it would take African Americans 228 years to reach the same wealth as white people. For immigrants from Latin America, the gap is even longer.

The economic crisis pushes us to bring all of our forces together. We have to find a new model that guarantees financial stability. In that search, we will need the potential of every citizen.

Try this. Watch the documentary Race: The Power of an Illusion, which shows how colonists built the idea of race in the United States. Do not tolerate jokes built on stereotypes. Learn about your neighborhood and the communities in it. If you spot a news story that could undermine stereotypes, share it with journalists. If the media represents social groups unfairly, contact the editors.

Made it to the end?
